definition: The L3 device (router or SVI) a host uses to reach other subnets.
Switch Virtual Interface — a routed VLAN interface on an L3 switch.
Intrusion Prevention/Detection System — identifies and blocks malicious traffic.
The router IP a host uses to reach destinations outside its subnet.
Tool listing each router hop toward a destination.
Temporary assignment of IP parameters from a DHCP server.
Service Set Identifier — the human-readable wireless network name.
Loss of RF signal strength caused by distance and obstacles.
Encryption used by WPA2 for confidential WLAN data.
Software/firmware that creates and runs virtual machines on hardware.
Cisco VRF without MPLS — separate routing tables on one router.
Deploying network services as virtualized software instead of dedicated appliances.
Two-tier campus design merging distribution and core layers.
Server-to-server traffic within a data center (vs north-south to clients).
Cloud model dividing security/ops duties between provider and customer.
Automatic crossover detection — modern ports adjust for straight or crossover cables.
Fiber with a small core for long-distance laser transmission.
Fiber with a larger core for shorter high-speed campus links.
Frame failed the cyclic redundancy check — indicates corruption in transit.
Collision detected after the slot time — classic duplex mismatch symptom.
Process where linked devices agree on highest common speed and duplex.
A switch's table of learned MAC-to-port (and VLAN) mappings, used to make forwarding decisions. Also called the CAM table.
Recording the source MAC, VLAN, and ingress port of every received frame into the MAC address table.
Removing a dynamically learned MAC entry after a period (default 300s) of no traffic from that address.
A frame whose destination MAC is present in the MAC address table — forwarded out one port only.
A unicast frame whose destination MAC is NOT in the table — flooded out all ports in the VLAN except the source.
Sending a frame out every port in a VLAN except the one it arrived on — used for unknown unicast, broadcast, and multicast.
32-bit value of contiguous 1s (network) then 0s (host) that divides an IPv4 address.
The /n notation giving the number of network bits in the mask.
256 minus the interesting mask octet; the increment between subnet boundaries.
The first address of a subnet (host bits all 0); identifies the subnet, not a host.
The last address of a subnet (host bits all 1); reaches all hosts in the subnet.
Variable-Length Subnet Masking — using different masks per subnet to match each subnet’s size.
Standard defining private IPv4 address space for internal networks.
Automatic self-assigned 169.254.x.x address when DHCP is unavailable.
Network Address Translation — maps private addresses to public for Internet access.
One of the eight 16-bit (4-hex-digit) groups in an IPv6 address.
The /n marking the network portion of an IPv6 address (usually /64 on LANs).
Stateless Address Autoconfiguration — host forms its address from the RA prefix.
Method to derive a 64-bit interface ID from a 48-bit MAC (insert FFFE, flip U/L bit).
The host portion (lower bits) of an IPv6 address, 64 bits with a /64 prefix.
Public, internet-routable IPv6 address (2000::/3).
Private IPv6 (FC00::/7, used as FD00::/8).
Auto-assigned FE80::/10 address used only on the local link.
One address on multiple nodes; delivered to the nearest.
FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx group used by Neighbor Discovery in place of ARP.
A logical Layer 2 broadcast domain configured on switches.
A switchport in one VLAN carrying untagged end-device traffic.
The untagged VLAN on a trunk; VLAN 1 by default.
A separate VLAN for IP-phone traffic on an access port.
A switch link carrying multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tagging.
The IEEE VLAN tagging standard; inserts a 4-byte tag with the VLAN ID.
The VLAN sent untagged on a trunk; must match both ends.
Dynamic Trunking Protocol; auto-negotiates trunk/access.
Cisco Discovery Protocol — L2 neighbor advertisement.
L2 protocol that blocks redundant paths to prevent loops.
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID; reference point of the tree.
A non-root switch’s lowest-cost port toward the root.
Feature letting access ports skip listening/learning.
Err-disables a PortFast port that receives a BPDU.
A letter (C/L/S/O/D/R/B) indicating how the router learned each route.
A route automatically installed when an interface is up/up with an IP — AD 0.
A /32 route for the router own interface IP — lets it process traffic destined to itself.
The default route (0.0.0.0/0) used when no more-specific route matches.
The rule that a router forwards using the matching route with the most specific (longest) prefix length.
A 0–255 trust rating for a routing source; lower is preferred. 255 means the route is never installed.
A routing protocol’s measure of how good a route is; only comparable within the same protocol.
The 0.0.0.0/0 route (gateway of last resort) that matches any destination but is the least specific.
A static route with a raised AD so it activates only when the preferred dynamic route is lost.
The router’s chosen default route, used when no more-specific route matches.
A manually configured routing table entry — AD 1 by default.
0.0.0.0/0 (or ::/0) — matches any destination when nothing more specific exists.
A static route with raised AD that installs only when the primary dynamic route fails.
The router must resolve the next-hop IP via another route before installing the static.
Open Shortest Path First — a link-state interior gateway protocol (AD 110).
Link-State Advertisement; the database of LSAs all routers in an area share.
Metric = reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth; lower is preferred.
A 32-bit ID for an OSPF router (highest loopback, else interface IP, or manual).
Designated/Backup Designated Router that reduce LSA flooding on multi-access links.
Network Address Translation — maps private to public IP addresses.
Port Address Translation (NAT overload) — many hosts share one public IP via ports.
The private IP of an inside host.
The public IP an inside host is translated to.
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three foundational security goals.
A weakness that could be exploited (unpatched software, misconfiguration).
Layered security controls so one failure does not compromise the entire network.
Access Control List — ordered permit/deny rules for filtering traffic.
Matches source IP only; placed near the destination.
Matches src/dst IP, protocol, and ports; placed near the source.
Inverse subnet mask used in ACLs (0 = match, 1 = ignore).
The unseen deny-all rule at the end of every ACL.
